content.text: Design Review Board Charter and Membership

A Design Review Board (DRB) is the governance mechanism by which an enterprise ensures that Claude integrations meet architectural, security, and operational standards before they are built and deployed. Without a DRB or an equivalent review process, individual teams make architectural decisions in isolation — and the consequence, at scale, is an estate of integrations with inconsistent security controls, incompatible data handling practices, redundant infrastructure, and undocumented design decisions that become hard to maintain.

The DRB is not a bureaucratic obstacle to speed. A well-run DRB accelerates delivery by catching problems at design time rather than at build time or, worse, in production. The cost of redesigning a data flow after a security review raises concerns is an order of magnitude higher than the cost of addressing the same concern in a design review. The charter and membership structures described in this document are designed to make the DRB efficient — not to maximise its footprint, but to maximise its value.

attrs.alt: DRB membership diagram showing Architecture Lead as chair, Security, Operations, AI/ML Platform as voting members, BU Sponsor as non-voting, plus three outcome states: Approved, Conditional, Rejected

content.text: The DRB Charter

The charter is the foundational document that establishes the DRB's authority, scope, and operating principles. It is approved by the enterprise architecture function or equivalent governance body, not by the DRB itself. The charter defines what the DRB is empowered to review and decide, preventing scope creep in both directions — teams should not be able to bypass the DRB for decisions within its scope, and the DRB should not be able to claim authority over decisions outside it.
Charter Template

DESIGN REVIEW BOARD CHARTER
[Organisation Name] — Claude Integration Programme
Version: [version] | Effective date: [date] | Approved by: [approving body]

1. PURPOSE
The Design Review Board (DRB) provides independent technical review of Claude
integration architectures before build begins. The DRB's purpose is to ensure
that integrations meet the organisation's standards for:
  - Architectural coherence (the design is internally consistent and compatible
    with the existing enterprise architecture)
  - Security and data handling (the integration handles data in accordance with
    applicable policies and regulatory requirements)
  - Operational readiness (the design can be monitored, maintained, and operated
    by the production operations function)
  - Cost viability (the projected cost at target scale is within approved budget)

2. AUTHORITY
  2.1 The DRB is authorised to approve, conditionally approve, or reject
      Claude integration architecture proposals within its defined scope.
  2.2 DRB decisions are binding on the submitting team. A REJECTED decision
      requires redesign; a CONDITIONAL approval requires the stated conditions
      to be met before build begins.
  2.3 The DRB does not have authority over implementation-level decisions
      (code structure, internal module design, variable naming, test strategy)
      unless those decisions have architectural implications.
  2.4 Appeals: A team that disagrees with a DRB decision may escalate to the
      Chief Architect within 10 business days of the decision. The Chief
      Architect's decision is final.

3. SCOPE
  Mandatory DRB review is required for:
  (a) Any new Claude integration moving to production
  (b) Any architecture change to an existing integration that modifies:
      the model selection or routing policy; the data flow or data handling
      boundary; the authentication or authorisation architecture; the
      system prompt governance policy; or the operational model
  (c) Any integration whose projected cost changes by more than 20% from
      the previously approved cost projection
  (d) Any integration where a security control is changed, removed, or added

  Exemptions: PoC and pilot-phase integrations are exempt from mandatory DRB
  review, but may elect to use the lightweight DRB process (section 7).

4. MEMBERSHIP
  [See section below for full membership specification]

5. MEETING CADENCE
  5.1 Regular DRB sessions are held bi-weekly, 90 minutes each.
  5.2 Submissions must be received no later than 5 business days before
      the session at which they will be reviewed.
  5.3 An expedited review track (10-business-day turnaround, including
      ad-hoc session if necessary) is available for high-priority submissions
      with CTO or equivalent sign-off.

6. DECISION STANDARDS
  6.1 APPROVED: The design meets all required standards. The submitting team
      may proceed to build without further DRB involvement.
  6.2 CONDITIONAL: The design meets required standards subject to stated
      conditions. Conditions are classified as:
      (a) Pre-build: must be addressed before build begins; the DRB chair
          confirms satisfaction of conditions before build permission is granted.
      (b) Pre-production: may be addressed during build but must be satisfied
          before the integration goes to production; the DRB chair confirms.
  6.3 REJECTED: The design does not meet required standards in a way that
      requires significant redesign. The DRB provides a written rejection
      statement identifying the specific shortfalls and, where possible,
      the direction of redesign that would meet the standards.

7. LIGHTWEIGHT PROCESS
  For PoC and pilot integrations that elect to use DRB review:
  - A lightweight submission (problem statement, proposed approach, known risks)
    replaces the full design package
  - Review is conducted asynchronously by the DRB chair and one additional member
  - The outcome is advisory, not binding
  - The lightweight review does not satisfy the mandatory review requirement for
    production deployment; a full DRB review is required before production

8. CHARTER REVIEW
  This charter is reviewed annually by the approving body and updated as
  required. Substantive changes to scope, authority, or membership structure
  require re-approval.
DRB Membership
Core Voting Members

The DRB has a small core of voting members who attend every session. Core membership is a commitment, not an ad-hoc invitation. Members who cannot attend a session must send a designated proxy who has the authority to vote on their behalf and who has reviewed the submissions in advance.
Role
Responsibility in DRB
Veto Rights
Commitment
Architecture Lead (Chair)
Runs the session; synthesises review comments into a decision; owns the decision record; has final say on architectural coherence
Yes — can block any approval on architectural coherence grounds
Every session; no proxy permitted for high-impact reviews
Security Representative
Reviews all security and data handling aspects; owns the security standard for Claude integrations
Yes — can block any approval on security grounds
Every session; designated proxy acceptable
Operations Lead
Reviews for operational feasibility; assesses runbook implications and monitoring requirements
No veto; required concurrence for APPROVED outcome
Every session; designated proxy acceptable
AI/ML Platform Representative
Reviews integration with the AI platform (shared infrastructure, model access, observability tools)
No veto; advisory on platform compatibility
Every session; designated proxy acceptable
Domain Expert Members
Domain expert members are invited on a per-submission basis when the submission involves their domain. They are not required to attend sessions where no relevant submission is on the agenda.
Data Privacy Officer (or delegate):
Invited for any submission involving personal data, regulated data categories, or cross-border data flows.
Infrastructure Lead:
Invited for submissions that change the hosting model, introduce new infrastructure components, or affect the shared platform capacity.
Legal/Compliance representative:
Invited for submissions with specific regulatory exposure (GDPR, HIPAA, FCA, etc.) or where AI-generated outputs have legal implications.
Product Owner of the submitting team:
Participates as a presenter and respondent to questions, not as a reviewer or voter.
Non-Voting Participants
Non-voting participants are informed of the DRB's decisions and may observe sessions, but do not vote and may not block an outcome:
Business Unit Sponsor:
The executive sponsor of the integration. Informed of outcomes; may raise concerns to the Chief Architect via the appeals process if they disagree with a decision.
PoC Team Lead:
Attends to present the design and respond to questions. Leaves the session before the deliberation phase.
Enterprise Architecture function:
Observer role; receives decision records for the architecture register.
The Submission Package

A DRB submission package is the documentation that allows the DRB to make a fully informed decision without requiring the submitting team to explain basics during the session. A complete package shifts the DRB session from information-gathering to review and decision-making — sessions that spend time on information-gathering are inefficient and produce lower-quality decisions.
Required Package Components
Architecture overview (one page): what the integration does, who uses it, how it connects to existing systems, and what the user experience looks like
Architecture diagram: a current diagram showing all components, data flows, and integration points (see ADR templates for diagram standards)
Security and data handling assessment: data classification, regulatory applicability, authentication mechanism, audit logging, and known residual risks
ADR index for this integration: all ADRs written for this integration, with their status and the ADRs relevant to this review flagged
Cost projection: cost per request at pilot scale and at target production scale, with the model and assumptions used for the projection
Operational readiness summary: monitoring approach, runbook status, and operations team readiness
Known limitations and open issues: an explicit list of known constraints, unresolved design questions, and risks the team is aware of
A submission package that omits any of these components will be returned to the submitting team for completion before the session. The DRB does not review incomplete packages.
The Pre-Submission Review

Before submitting to the DRB, the architecture lead should conduct an informal pre-submission review with the submitting team. The pre-submission review is not a gate — it is a coaching conversation that helps the team prepare a stronger submission and identify issues that would otherwise surface as objections during the DRB session.
Does the architecture diagram reflect the actual design, or an idealised version? DRB reviewers who discover gaps between the diagram and the implementation lose confidence in the submission.
Are the ADRs current? An ADR that says 'we will use Sonnet' when the implementation actually uses Haiku is not a documentation gap — it is a governance failure.
Is the cost projection based on actual pilot data, or on theoretical usage estimates? Projections based on actual data are defensible; projections based on assumed query volume are not.

Has the security representative been consulted informally before the session? A security concern raised for the first time during the DRB session almost always leads to a CONDITIONAL or REJECTED outcome.
Running an Effective DRB Session
Session Structure

STANDARD DRB SESSION AGENDA (90 minutes)

00:00 — 00:05   Opening: chair confirms quorum, reviews agenda
00:05 — 00:25   Presentation: submitting team presents the design (15 min)
                 followed by clarifying questions (5 min, factual only)
00:25 — 00:55   Review: DRB members share observations by domain
                 Security representative (10 min)
                 Operations lead (8 min)
                 AI/ML Platform representative (7 min)
                 Architecture lead synthesis (5 min)
00:55 — 01:05   Submitting team response to review observations (10 min)
01:05 — 01:20   Deliberation: submitting team leaves; DRB discusses
                 and reaches a decision (15 min)
01:20 — 01:25   Decision communicated to submitting team; conditions
                 (if any) stated; next steps confirmed (5 min)
01:25 — 01:30   Decision recorded; session closed
Decision Documentation

Every DRB decision is documented in a decision record within 2 business days of the session. The decision record is the official record of the DRB's finding and is referenced in the integration's ADR index.

DRB DECISION RECORD
Integration: [name] | Review date: YYYY-MM-DD
Submission version: [version] | DRB session: [session ID]
Submitting team lead: [name]

DECISION: APPROVED | CONDITIONAL | REJECTED

[If APPROVED:]
The design meets all required standards. The submitting team may proceed to build.
Approved by: [Architecture lead], [Security representative], [Operations lead]

[If CONDITIONAL:]
The design meets required standards subject to the following conditions:

PRE-BUILD CONDITIONS (must be resolved before build begins):
  C1: [condition description]
      Owner: [DRB member or submitting team member responsible for verifying]
  C2: [condition description]

PRE-PRODUCTION CONDITIONS (must be resolved before production deployment):
  C3: [condition description]

Conditional approval granted by: [Architecture lead]
Conditions verified by: [DRB chair] (verification date: [date])

[If REJECTED:]
The design does not meet required standards in the following areas:

  R1: [shortfall description, reference to standard not met]
  R2: [shortfall description]

Direction for redesign:
  [Specific guidance on what needs to change for the redesign to be acceptable,
   without prescribing the exact solution — that is the submitting team's role]

Rejected by: [Architecture lead] | Escalation deadline: [date + 10 business days]
DRB Health Metrics
A DRB that is not monitored can drift into ineffectiveness over time. The following metrics indicate whether the DRB is operating as intended:
Metric
Target
Warning Signal
Average session duration
< 90 minutes
> 90 min consistently: submissions are incomplete or review is not focused
Pre-submission completion rate
> 90% complete on first submission
< 90%: pre-submission coaching is ineffective or teams are rushing submissions
Conditional approval rate
< 40% of submissions
> 40%: submissions are systematically underprepared; earlier coaching needed
Rejection rate
5–15% of submissions
< 5% may indicate rubber-stamping; > 20% may indicate submissions are reaching DRB too early
Time from submission to decision
< 15 business days
> 15 days: backlog or scheduling problem; may indicate DRB capacity constraint
Condition close rate within deadline
< 95%: teams are not prioritising condition resolution; escalation process needed
Summary

The Design Review Board charter establishes the DRB's authority, scope, membership, and operating procedures. The charter is the document that gives the DRB legitimacy — without it, the DRB is an informal review that teams can circumvent. The membership structure balances expertise coverage with session efficiency: a small core of voting members who attend every session, supplemented by domain experts invited for relevant submissions.

The submission package and session structure are designed to make the DRB's time as productive as possible. A complete submission package shifts the session from information-gathering to review; a structured agenda prevents one reviewer's concerns from consuming the session. The decision record provides an auditable trail of every DRB finding, and the health metrics keep the DRB accountable for its own efficiency. Together, these elements make the DRB a governance mechanism that accelerates delivery by catching architectural problems at design time rather than in production.
